title: Salt Sugar Fat: How the Food Giants Hooked Us
rationale: Salt Sugar Fat is the defining investigative exposé of the processed food industry's deliberate manipulation of three core ingredients to engineer addictive products. Michael Moss, a Pulitzer Prize-winning New York Times investigative reporter, secured unprecedented access to industry insiders, internal documents, and food science laboratories to build a damning case that the obesity epidemic was not an accident of consumer choice but a calculated outcome of corporate strategy. The book spent over six months on the New York Times bestseller list, won the James Beard Foundation Book Award for Writing and Literature, and has been translated into 22 languages. Published February 26, 2013 by Random House (hardcover, 446 pages, ISBN 9781400069804). The paperback edition (2014, Random House Trade Paperbacks, ISBN 9780812982190, 480 pages) includes an updated afterword. The book is structured in three parts covering sugar (chapters 1-4), fat (chapters 5-8), and salt (chapters 9-11), with an epilogue on consumer action. Moss's reporting built on his Pulitzer Prize-winning 2009 New York Times series on food safety that popularized the term 'pink slime.'
